mnemonics.title: HEART Framework
mnemonics.content: Remember HEART: Happiness (attitude), Engagement (interaction), Adoption (new users), Retention (returning users), Task Success (efficiency) - like a beating heart keeping UX alive!

mnemonics.title: SUS Score Interpretation
mnemonics.content: SUS Scoring: Below 50 = Poor, 50-70 = OK, Above 70 = Good, Above 80 = Excellent. Think: 68 is the dividing line between below and above average.

mnemonics.title: NPS Calculation
mnemonics.content: NPS = Promoters (9-10) % minus Detractors (0-6) %. Ignore Passives (7-8). Remember: Only extremes matter!

mnemonics.title: Usability Metrics Types
mnemonics.content: Three pillars of Usability: Effectiveness (can they do it?), Efficiency (how fast?), Satisfaction (do they like it?). Like a triple crown!

acronyms.usage: 10-question survey measuring perceived usability, scored 0-100, above 68 is above average
Measures loyalty by likelihood to recommend, range -100 to +100
Measures immediate satisfaction with specific interactions, typically 1-5 scale

acronyms.expansion: Happiness, Engagement, Adoption, Retention, Task Success
acronyms.usage: Google's framework for measuring UX quality at scale
Comparing two design versions to determine better performer using statistical significance

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a thermometer: 0-50 (cold/poor), 50-70 (lukewarm/average), 70-90 (warm/good), 90-100 (hot/excellent). 68°F is room temperature - average!
Picture a 3-zone traffic light: Green (Promoters 9-10), Yellow (Passives 7-8), Red (Detractors 0-6). Green minus Red = NPS!
Think of a tree: Roots = Business Goals, Trunk = UX Goals, Branches = Metrics, Leaves = Targets. All connected!
Success Rate = ✓Completed / ✗Total Attempts × 100. Like a passing grade in school!

analogies.analogy: UX goals are like a destination on a GPS - they tell you where you want to go before you start the journey.
Metrics are like the dashboard instruments in a car - speedometer, fuel gauge, temperature - showing how you're doing during the drive.
Targets are like setting a speed limit or arrival time - specific numbers you want to achieve or beat.
SUS is like a school grade - 68 is a C (average), above 80 is an A, below 50 is a failing grade.
Bounce rate is like a store where customers walk in, look around, and immediately walk out without buying anything.
A/B testing is like a taste test - giving half customers recipe A and half recipe B to see which one gets more thumbs up.
